Streetcar

Homepage Bus Bus Inside Streetcar Streetcar Inside Subway Subway Inside

Hello, thanks for choosing the most modern form of transit around the world, swipe left to contemporary technology. In my opinion, the streetcar is the coolest form of transportation. It’s convenient, easy to find on any major street, and simple to board. It also connects with subway stations along its route, making transfers straightforward. It’s no surprise that one of North America’s largest and oldest streetcar networks is also one of the most efficient ways to get around.

The streetcar goes along major roads, and the signage should have a 3-digit number like 505-Dundas. The arrival of each streetcar, without traffic, is usually 10 minutes apart. There is also a night option. Don’t worry about the Streetcar missing you; they always have to stop, unless the driver forgets.

Streetcars carry more riders and offer more seating, have an accessibility ramp, and are safe because the driver has to get out and man the ramp, ensuring no cars drive by. Because of its size, there are multiple ways to access it, which helps traffic flow more smoothly. Of all the transportation options, I would say it's the least rocky.

Once the streetcar stopped and the doors opened, for love of God, look out for drivers when getting on and off. Some Toronto drivers don’t have eyes. When there’s an accident or road work ahead, the streetcar stops running along that path and detours, skipping multiple stops. Slow, some considered even slower than the bus

3. Click the round button on the door to open it

4. When the doors close and the streetcar moves, you can’t open the doors and don’t rush for it, as drivers are already driving forward.

Strategies:
1. Don’t stand in the road, before the streetcar stops, or when cars are nonexistent

2. Stand to the side of the doors and wait for people to get off
